title: Partner Notification & HIV
desc: A 29-year-old woman newly diagnosed with HIV begs you not to tell her fiancé, who she says will leave and shame her family.

law.text: Physicians must protect patient confidentiality while balancing prevention of foreseeable serious harm to identifiable third parties when no safer alternative exists.

complications.title: Mother demands silence
complications.premise: Salma's mother threatens to withdraw support if the fiancé is told.
complications.liveLine: Her mother pulls you aside: "If you destroy her engagement, everyone will know — you swore to secrecy. Write that he doesn't need to know."

complications.title: Fiancé calls the ward
complications.premise: The partner asks why Salma is still admitted.
complications.liveLine: 📞 "Doctor, we're getting married next month — is she hiding something serious? Say yes or no."

complications.title: Possible recent exposure
complications.premise: History suggests unprotected contact within 72 hours.
complications.liveLine: Nursing notes: intimate contact two days ago — PEP might still be possible for the partner, but only if he knows and presents.

complications.title: Infection control query
complications.premise: Hospital wants contact tracing form completed.
complications.liveLine: ⚠️ IPC officer: "We need declaration for partner notification protocol — leadership expects completed forms today for reporting."

steps.msg: Doctor, please... do not tell him. I will lose everything — my family, my future. I will take every pill; I promise. Just keep this only between us.
steps.q: What is the best first response?
steps.hint: Balance autonomy, confidentiality, and preventable harm:

choices.label: Explore risks & support for disclosure
choices.reaction: I'm still terrified — but I hear you won't shame me.

choices.label: Promise permanent secrecy
choices.reaction: Thank you... but part of me knows I'm asking you to hide danger.

choices.label: Insist you will inform him today
choices.reaction: Then I will never trust you — I might leave care entirely.

choices.label: Refer only to legal services
choices.reaction: I needed my doctor's voice, not a form.

steps.msg: Salma admits she is not sure her fiancé was tested — they were intimate recently. She asks: "Isn't there a way he can be protected without everyone knowing I am sick?"
steps.q: The clinical picture allows counseling but partner health is at stake. What do you do next?
steps.hint: Think dual protection: trust in care and prevention of harm:

choices.label: Offer mediated / joint counseling options
choices.reaction: Maybe… if someone helps us talk, it won't be only my shame.

choices.label: Explore agency partner services / anonymous alerts
choices.reaction: If the system can warn without my name attached…

choices.label: Document refusal & discharge
choices.reaction: So I leave with this secret forever? I'm not sure that's care.

steps.msg: She agrees to gradual disclosure with your team and a counselor next week — but asks you not to chart the fiancé's name until then. Nursing rounds begin in five minutes.
steps.q: How do you close this ethically?
steps.hint: Charting must be accurate; timing of partner notification may be staged:

choices.label: Chart truthfully + timed partner plan
choices.reaction: I'm scared, but this feels honest.

choices.label: Omit partner from chart for now
choices.reaction: I asked you to hide truth — now I don't know what's recorded.

choices.label: Same-day ethics consult
choices.reaction: If experts agree on a path, my family might listen.

debrief.summary: HIV confidentiality is profound but not absolute when a partner faces concrete preventable harm. The ethical apex combines empathic exploration, accurate records, and a supported disclosure or prevention pathway.
points: Start with trust-building — coercive disclosure destroys care engagement.
Document clinical facts honestly; secrecy is not the same as lying in the chart.
When feasible, mediated disclosure or public-health partner services protect both dignity and prevention.
Autonomy includes helping patients foresee consequences of silence, not only obeying requests.
